So Facebook already knows from your product catalog which products people are more likely to be viewing. And that's part of the reason why DPA at the cold audience level, I think works fairly well, because Facebook actually has data on your cold audience that you don't have. ## [39:32 - 42:35]

no one does. Their view of scaling is use average bid and just keep increasing budget. So when they give you that graph, they're telling you, if you went from $500 to $2,000 a day, you know, these are the conversions you can expect. Reality, and you'll know if you went from $500 to $2,000 in a day, it would screw up the option, your results will be all over the place, but they would settle. This is the thing, like if you're willing to wait for 24 hours, 48 hours of making losses, it will settle. And so what Facebook say makes sense, but this is the gripe I have with Facebook is a lot of the reps, they've never actually managed budget before, they've never had performance challenges of actually maintaining profitability, looking after your spend and things like that. That this is best practice and this is what you should do. But in reality, you know, whether you're running your own account or running a client's account, you make losses after 24 hours, you're panicking, you make losses after 48 hours, you know, the stuff's going to hit the fan. And so we can't as people on the ground afford to make those losses. It's like the bully method, for example, which is well known, where you go $1,000 and you will make you make losses for the first day, but you have to hold your nerve, because at some point you will bully other people out of the auction, and then you'll start to make profit, but not everyone can do that. Does that have a positive or negative impact? So like for example, if you've got a product that's $20 to $40 and you're making tweaks in your cold, you'll know quite quickly whether that's converting into purchases and the roast that's falling out. that registration is going to be within our range as well.
